content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

March 7, 2014


Lynn Schenk
3580 Carmel Mountain Rd., Suite 300
San Diego, CA 92130

Re:     Your Request for Advice
        Our File No. A-14-004

Dear Ms. Schenk:

        This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the gift provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ This letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political
Practices Commission (the "Commission") does not act as a finder of fact when rendering
advice. (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71.)

QUESTION

        If AT&T Corporation ("AT&T") relocates an AT&T pedestal junction box located on
your property to another part of your property at your request without charge, will you receive a
gift from AT&T for the value of the services?

CONCLUSION

        Generally, goods or services received by a public official without cost are considered
reportable gifts to the official and are subject to the $440 gift limit. The Act provides an
exception if the "gift" is a rebate or discount available for no charge regardless of official
position. We do not have sufficient information to determine if the exception applies in your
case given our inability to obtain further information from AT&T regarding this matter.




¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

Our File No. A-14-004
Page No. 2

FACTS

You have provided the following facts:

• You currently serve as a board member on the California High Speed Rail Authority (the
"Authority").

• You own a home and reside in the City of San Diego.

• In 1963, the predecessor company of AT&T placed a pedestal junction box in the
backyard of a residence that you later purchased. The predecessor company and AT&T
have maintained access to the box by an easement agreement.

• Currently, access to the box by AT&T employees and contractors requires them to
contact you to make an appointment. In order to simplify access to the box, you offered a
different site on your property to AT&T for the box. There is a part of your property that
is accessible from the street without the need for prearranged access to open gates. The
new location would make access completely available and unfettered for the AT&T.

• Initially, AT&T agreed to move the box to the new location. However, recently, they
determined that relocating the junction box for you without charge would constitute a
gift. You described their communication as follows:

"All of a sudden, I received a call and then a letter saying that
because I am a member of the California High Speed Rail Authority,
AT&T has determined that relocating the junction box would constitute a
gift to me under the Political Reform Act of 1974 as a result of my
position on the Authority Board. The letter continued to say that 'outside
of my request, there is no significant business reason for AT&T to relocate
the box.'

"This is twisting the entire scenario, as AT&T is the main
beneficiary of my 'gift' to them of improved access to the junction box.
Thus, the company contends that the cost of relocation constitutes a 'gift'
to me and I should underwrite the cost of the relocation to eliminate this
issue."

You have asked whether AT&T's moving the box to a different location on your property
to allow AT&T unfettered access to it, at your request and without charge, would constitute a gift
under the Act.

Our File No. A-14-004
Page No. 3


ANALYSIS

Subject to certain exceptions (see Section 82028(b) and Regulations 18942, 18944 and
18950 - 18950.3) the term "gift" is defined in Section 82028(a) as:

"Any payment [2] that confers a personal benefit on the recipient, to the
extent that consideration of equal or greater value is not received and includes a
rebate or discount in the price of anything of value unless the rebate or discount is
made in the regular course of business to members of the public without regard to
official status."

In other words, if the decision to relocate the box without cost under your facts was a
personal benefit to you that AT&T would not offer its other customers, this would be considered
a gift to you.

Conversely, the gift definition does not apply to "a rebate or discount in the price of
anything of value" if "the rebate or discount is made in the regular course of business to
members of the public without regard to official status." For example, in In re Russel (1975) I
FPPC Ops. 191, in response to a question about a discount offered by the Holiday Inn to all state
employees, the Commission stated:

"[D]isclosure is required so that assets and income of public officials
which may be materially affected by their official actions will be disclosed.
Section 81002(d). Discounts are included ... so that officials who are offered
special reductions when they purchase goods and services will disclose those
transactions for public scrutiny. However, many discounts are offered to all
members of the public and do not create any potential for improper influence.
Requiring the disclosure of all discounts would impose burdensome reporting
requirements without serving a legitimate public purpose."

After requesting further information from you, as well as from AT&T, we are unable to
determine whether, factually, the decision to relocate the box without cost to you under your facts
is a personal benefit to you that AT&T would not offer its other customers in a similar
situation, or whether it is a customary service provided to AT&T's customers. Absent an
exception, the movement of the box without cost would be a gift.

If the benefit is a gift, the following rules apply:

• The Act imposes reporting obligations on certain public officials requiring that any gifts
aggregating to $50 or more from the same source received during the calendar year be
disclosed on the officials' Statements of Economic Interests (FPPC Form 700) so that the

[2] Section 82044 defines "payment," in part, as any "rendering of ... services or anything else of value,
whether tangible or intangible."

Our File No. A-14-004
Page No. 4

public is made aware of any potential influences from gifts. Reporting requirements
apply to, among other officials, all officials listed in Section 87200 (including members
of the Authority).

• The Act places limitations on the acceptance by certain public officials of gifts from non-
lobbyists. The current limit is $440 from a single source in a calendar year. (Section
89503; Regulation 18940.2.) The limit applies to, among other officials, all officials
listed in Section 87200 (including members of the Authority). Gifts received that have a
value greater than $440, may be paid down to the permissible gift limit within 30 days of
receipt.

• The Act places limitations on gifts to certain state public officials that are made or
arranged by a state lobbyist or lobbying firm registered to lobby that official's agency.
The limit is $10 per calendar month. (See Sections 86201, 86203 and 86204.)

• The Act prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using his
or her official position to influence a governmental decision involving the donor of a gift
or gifts with an aggregate value of $440 or more provided to, received by, or promised to
the official within the 12 months prior to the date of the official's participation in the
decision. (Sections 87100 and 87103(e); Regulations 18700 and 18703.4.)

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Zackery P. Morazzini
General Counsel

By: John W. Wallace
Assistant General Counsel
Legal Division
